quiz.title: Unit 9, Quiz 4: GDP, Inflation, Unemployment, and PPF Calculations
quiz.subtitle: National Income Accounting, Price Indices, Labour Force, and Production Possibilities
quiz.subject: AP Macroeconomics
quiz.topic: GDP Calculation, Real vs. Nominal GDP, CPI and Inflation, Unemployment Rate, Labour Force Participation Rate, PPF and Opportunity Cost

questions.question: Country X has the following data: C = $800, I = $200, G = $150, Exports = $100, Imports = $120. What is the GDP?
When the economy is producing at potential GDP, which of the following is TRUE?
options: There is no unemployment in the economy
There is room for growth, all things being equal
questions.explanation: At potential GDP, the economy is at full employment but still experiences natural unemployment (frictional and structural), so Option A is false. 'All things being equal' means no change in resources or technology, so there is no room for additional growth without expanding capacity — Option B is false. Inflation is not inevitable at potential GDP; prices can be stable when the economy is operating at its sustainable capacity — Option C is false. Therefore, none of the options are true.

questions.question: Household income = $900, household savings = $150, taxes = $100, government spending = $120. No variable has a zero value. Does the circular flow balance?
questions.explanation: To determine if the circular flow balances, we need all leakages (savings, taxes, imports) and all injections (investment, government spending, exports). Only savings ($150), taxes ($100), and government spending ($120) are given. Imports, investment, and exports are missing but cannot be zero. Without these values, we cannot determine whether leakages equal injections.

questions.question: The Nominal GDP = $2,500; GDP deflator = 125. What is the Real GDP?
The CPI last year = 200; CPI this year = 210. What is the inflation rate?
questions.explanation: Inflation rate = [(CPI this year − CPI last year) ÷ CPI last year] × 100 = [(210 − 200) ÷ 200] × 100 = (10 ÷ 200) × 100 = 0.05 × 100 = 5%.

questions.question: Labour force = 1,200; employed = 1,080; unemployed = 120. What is the unemployment rate?
The labour force participation rate includes all the following EXCEPT
questions.explanation: The labour force participation rate measures the percentage of the working-age population that is either employed or actively seeking employment. Discouraged workers, who have given up looking for work, are not counted in the labour force. First-time job seekers are included if they are actively seeking work.

questions.question: A population contains the following: under 16 years: 2250, 16–18 years: 540, 18–65 years: 1120, >65: 90, unemployed workers: 320, discouraged workers: 120. Assuming the retirement age is 65, what is the labour force participation rate?
questions.explanation: Working-age population (16–65) = 540 + 1120 = 1660. Employed = working-age population − unemployed − discouraged = 1660 − 320 − 120 = 1220. Labour force = employed + unemployed = 1220 + 320 = 1540. LFPR = (1540 ÷ 1660) × 100 ≈ 92.8%, which rounds to 93%.

questions.question: A country reports: Consumption = $2,500B, Government purchases = $800B, Private investment = $600B, Exports = $400B, Imports = $350B, Corporate profits = $200B, Transfer payments = $150B, Depreciation = $100B. What is the GDP?
questions.explanation: GDP (expenditure approach) = C + I + G + (X − M) = $2,500 + $600 + $800 + ($400 − $350) = $2,500 + $600 + $800 + $50 = $3,950 billion. Corporate profits, transfer payments, and depreciation are not used in this approach.

questions.question: An economy produces only cars and computers. Its PPF is: (0 cars, 500 computers), (100, 480), (200, 440), (300, 360), (400, 200), (500, 0). Which statement is true?
options: The opportunity cost of producing the 200th car is 40 computers.
The opportunity cost of producing a car increases as more cars are produced.
The economy can produce 300 cars and 400 computers.
Producing 200 cars and 440 computers is inefficient.
questions.explanation: B is correct because the PPF is concave: moving from 100–200 cars costs 40 computers (0.4 per car), from 200–300 costs 80 computers (0.8 per car) — increasing opportunity cost. A is false because the 200th car costs 0.4 computers, not 40. C is unattainable (outside PPF). D is efficient (on PPF).

scoreMessages.90: Excellent mastery of GDP calculations, price indices, labour force concepts, and PPF analysis.
scoreMessages.70: Good understanding; review the distinction between nominal and real GDP, discouraged workers, and PPF opportunity cost calculations.
scoreMessages.50: Needs focus on GDP expenditure approach, CPI inflation formula, and labour force participation rate calculations.
scoreMessages.0: Revisit: GDP components, real vs. nominal GDP, unemployment rate formula, and PPF interpretation.
